claim: Eval platforms are not just test runners but systems that require shared definitions of 'good', reliable data pipelines, labeling workflows, versioning, and trust across teams.
actionable: When building or adopting an eval platform, plan for multi-team alignment on scoring definitions, data governance, and version control, not just technical infrastructure.
source_title: Why building eval platforms is hard

claim: Spreadsheet-based evals (Google Sheets, Excel) are quick to start but scale poorly because they don't encourage experimentation, make cross-experiment comparison cumbersome, and don't scale human scoring.
actionable: Move beyond spreadsheets early; invest in a proper eval tool or platform once you have >10 regular eval runs to enable non-technical team members to participate.
source_title: Why building eval platforms is hard

claim: Agent traces are fundamentally different from application logs: they're large (10-20 MB per span), semi-structured, high-velocity, and require both low-latency reads and aggregate analytics.
actionable: When building observability for agents, don't use traditional SQL databases or application logging stacks; use systems designed for large semi-structured data (e.g., data warehouses with full-text search).
source_title: Why building eval platforms is hard

claim: The eval-observability flywheel: collect production traces → run evals against production examples → improve agent → redeploy. This loop is critical for continuous quality improvement.
actionable: Set up automatic pipelines to funnel production traces into offline evals; use real user interactions to discover failure modes and drive agent improvements.
source_title: Why building eval platforms is hard

claim: The best way to find agent failure modes is via production trace data; focus eval design on these real failure modes rather than synthetic scenarios.
actionable: Analyze production traces to identify failure modes, then design scoring functions that detect those specific failure modes in offline evals.
source_title: Why building eval platforms is hard

claim: Building eval platforms is primarily a data systems problem, not a UI/UX problem; the hard part is managing, querying, and analyzing large semi-structured traces at scale.
actionable: When evaluating eval platform tools, prioritize backend data architecture over UI features; ensure the platform can query and aggregate traces efficiently.
source_title: Why building eval platforms is hard
